3. Using BIOS Updater

BIOS Updater can also be used to update the BIOS in a DOS environment. This method requires preparing a USB flash drive with the BIOS file and BIOS Updater tool, then booting into DOS and running the updater command. This option is generally used when you specifically need to update from a DOS environment.,['Manual25_60', 'Manual25_61']

To create a RAID array on this motherboard, please prepare two or more SATA hard drives/SSDs and connect them to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s ports on the motherboard. The Intel Z170 chipset supports RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 5, and RAID 10 through these SATA connectors.

<PIC>

Before starting, please note:
- Back up any important data first. Creating RAID may erase data on the selected drives.
- Use the Intel SATA connectors shown above, not third-party storage ports.
- Make sure the drives you want to use do not already contain an existing RAID volume.
- RAID 10 requires four drives.

1. Enter the BIOS, then set SATA mode to RAID.

The SATA mode is set to AHCI by default. To create a RAID array, go to Advanced > SATA Configuration, then set SATA Mode Selection to RAID. Save the setting if prompted.

2. Open EZ Tuning Wizard.

In the BIOS screen, press F11 on the keyboard, or click EZ Tuning Wizard to open the wizard.

<PIC>

3. Select RAID.

In EZ Tuning Wizard, click RAID, then click Yes to enable RAID mode.

4. Select the drives.

The available HDDs/SSDs will be displayed. Confirm that the drives you want to use are listed, then click Next.

<PIC>

5. Choose the RAID usage type.

Select the storage function that matches your purpose, then click Next:
- Easy Backup: focuses on data redundancy.
- Super Speed: focuses on performance.

<PIC>

6. Choose the RAID level.

If you select Easy Backup, choose one of the following:
- Easy Backup (RAID 1): mirrors data between drives for redundancy.
- Easy Backup (RAID 10): combines mirroring and striping, but requires four drives.

<PIC>

If you select Super Speed, choose one of the following:
- Super Speed (RAID 0): improves performance by striping data across drives, but does not provide redundancy.
- Super Speed (RAID 5): provides both striping and parity, requiring at least three drives.

<PIC>

7. Confirm the RAID creation.

After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue. Wait for the setup process to complete.

8. Restart the system.

When the RAID setup is finished, click Yes to exit, then click OK to reset the system. After rebooting, the RAID volume should be available for operating system installation or disk management, depending on your setup.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_71', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

To install OS 7 on a 100 Series motherboard using a SATA optical drive and a USB installation device, you can use the SATA ODD to load the USB 3.0 driver automatically while booting the OS installer from USB.

Before you start, prepare the following:
• 1 x motherboard support DVD
• 1 x OS 7 installation source
• 1 x SATA ODD
• 1 x USB device, either a USB ODD or a USB storage device

If you are using a USB storage device, it should have at least 8 GB capacity. Formatting the USB storage device before use is recommended, and please back up any important data first.

Steps:

1. Prepare the OS 7 installation media.
   Insert the OS 7 installation DVD into a USB ODD, or use another working system to copy all files from the OS 7 installation DVD to a USB storage device.

2. Connect the USB ODD or USB storage device to the 100 Series motherboard system.

3. Insert the motherboard support DVD into the SATA ODD connected to the same system. This DVD will be used to load the required USB 3.0 driver during installation.

4. Power on the system. During POST, press F8 to open the boot device selection screen.

<PIC>

5. In the boot menu, select the USB ODD or USB storage device that contains the OS 7 installation files, then press Enter to boot from it.

6. During the installation startup process, the USB 3.0 driver will be loaded automatically from the support DVD in the SATA ODD.

<PIC>

When the “Setup is starting...” screen appears, it indicates that the USB 3.0 driver has been loaded correctly and the installation process can continue.

7. Follow the on-screen prompts to complete the OS 7 installation.

Note: Keep both the USB installation device and the SATA ODD with the support DVD connected during the early installation process, so the system can access the installer and load the required USB 3.0 driver properly.,['Manual25_87', 'Manual25_88']

To secure the motherboard to the chassis, first make sure the power cord is unplugged and the motherboard is positioned correctly inside the case. The side with the external I/O ports should face the rear of the chassis.

Align the motherboard with the chassis standoffs, then install nine screws into the screw holes marked with red circles in the image below. Tighten each screw evenly until it is secure.

<PIC>

Important: Do not over-tighten the screws. Excessive force may bend or damage the motherboard. The screws only need to be snug enough to hold the board firmly in place.,['Manual25_6']

To prepare for installing the Central Processing Unit (CPU) on the motherboard, please complete the following checks and precautions first:

<PIC>

1. Disconnect power completely
• Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any motherboard component.
• Before installing or removing any component, make sure the ATX power supply is switched off, or that the power cord is detached from the power supply.
• This helps prevent electric shock, short circuits, and possible damage to the motherboard or connected components.

2. Protect components from static electricity
• Before handling the CPU, motherboard, or other parts, wear a grounded wrist strap if available.
• If you do not have one, touch a safely grounded metal object, such as the power supply case, before handling components.
• Handle components only by their edges, and avoid touching ICs, socket contacts, pins, or circuitry.

3. Keep the work area safe
• Place the motherboard on a stable, clean, non-conductive surface.
• Keep screws, paper clips, staples, and other small metal objects away from the CPU socket, slots, connectors, and circuitry.
• If you remove any component, place it on a grounded antistatic pad or in its antistatic bag.

<PIC>

This motherboard uses a surface-mount CPU socket LGA1151, designed for 6th Generation processors. Before installing the CPU, confirm the following:

• Use only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket.
• Do not install CPUs designed for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets into this LGA1151 socket, as they are not compatible and may damage the socket or CPU.
• Check that the PnP protective cap is present on the socket before installation.
• Inspect the CPU socket contacts carefully. If the PnP cap is missing, or if you notice bent socket contacts or damage to the socket, cap, or motherboard components, contact your retailer before continuing.
• Keep the PnP cap after installing the CPU/motherboard, as it may be required if the motherboard needs service or return handling later.

Once these preparations are complete and the socket has been checked, you can proceed with the CPU installation steps.,['Manual25_5', 'Manual25_9']

You can check the system memory information for this motherboard in two ways: by checking the motherboard memory specifications and slot configuration, and by viewing the detected memory information in BIOS.

This motherboard is equipped with four DDR4 DIMM memory slots. The slots are labeled DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. Please use DDR4 memory only, because DDR4 modules have a different notch position from DDR, DDR2, and DDR3 modules. DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 memory modules cannot be installed in these DDR4 slots.

<PIC>

For supported capacity, this motherboard can use 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB unbuffered non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs. With four slots installed, the maximum supported system memory is up to 64 GB. It is also recommended to use DIMMs with voltage below 1.65 V to help protect the CPU.

For best compatibility, please follow the recommended installation positions below:

<PIC>

Recommended memory installation:

• 1 DIMM: Install one memory module. Although one module can work in any slot as single-channel memory, DIMM_A2 is recommended for better compatibility.

• 2 DIMMs: Install the two memory modules in the same-color slots as one dual-channel pair. DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2 are recommended for better compatibility.

• 4 DIMMs: Install memory modules in all four slots: DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. This works as two dual-channel pairs.

Additional memory notes:

• You can install different memory sizes in Channel A and Channel B. The system will use the matching capacity between the two channels in dual-channel mode, and any extra capacity from the larger channel will run in single-channel mode.

• For best stability and compatibility, it is recommended to use memory modules with the same CAS latency, and preferably modules from the same vendor and same kit/version.

• If you install 4 GB or more memory, a 64-bit operating system is recommended so the system can fully use the installed memory.

• Some high-frequency memory may run at a lower default speed because the default frequency depends on the module’s SPD information. If needed, memory frequency can be adjusted in BIOS under the overclocking-related settings.

You can also check the detected system memory directly in BIOS. After entering BIOS Advanced Mode, open the Main menu. This screen shows basic system information, including BIOS, processor, and memory information.

<PIC>

For more detailed memory module information, go to the SPD Information section in the BIOS Tool menu. It displays the Serial Presence Detect information for the memory module installed in the selected slot. The available slot options are DIMM_A1, DIMM_B1, DIMM_A2, and DIMM_B2.,['Manual25_18', 'Manual25_19', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector, marked as the 10-1 pin COM header on the motherboard, is an internal connector used to add a traditional serial/COM port to the system. It is not the external DB-9 port itself; instead, you connect a compatible serial port module cable to this onboard COM header, then secure the module’s DB-9 bracket to an available slot opening at the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

As shown above, the COM connector is located along the bottom edge of the motherboard. The enlarged pinout shows the signal assignments for the 10-1 pin header:

- PIN 1: DCD
- RXD
- TXD
- DTR
- GND
- DSR
- RTS
- CTS
- RI

When connecting the module, align the cable correctly with PIN 1 on the COM header to avoid incorrect wiring. The serial port module/bracket is an optional accessory and is purchased separately.

You can also enable or configure this COM port in BIOS:

1. Go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration.
2. Set Serial Port to [On] to enable the COM port, or [Off] to disable it.
3. When Serial Port is set to [On], the Change Settings option becomes available. This allows you to select the COM port base address and IRQ setting.

Available Change Settings options include:

- [IO=3F8h; IRQ=4]
- [IO=2F8h; IRQ=3]
- [IO=3E8h; IRQ=4]
- [IO=2E8h; IRQ=3]

In short, the 10-1 pin COM connector is used when you need legacy serial communication support, such as for certain industrial devices, POS equipment, debugging tools, or other RS-232 serial devices, provided that a compatible COM port module is installed.,['Manual25_39']

The TPM connector, labeled as the 14-1 pin TPM header on the motherboard, is used to connect a Trusted Platform Module (TPM). A TPM helps protect the system by securely storing keys, digital certificates, passwords, and other sensitive data. It can also help improve network security, protect digital identities, and verify platform integrity.

<PIC>

In the image, the TPM connector location is highlighted on the bottom edge of the motherboard, and the enlarged diagram shows the 14-1 pin layout. When connecting a TPM module, align the module with the missing/key pin position and the marked PIN 1 orientation shown in the diagram to avoid incorrect installation.

Please power off the system and unplug the power cord before installing or removing the TPM module.,['Manual25_40']

The Thermal sensor connector, labeled 2-pin T_SENSOR or T_SENSOR1 on the motherboard, is used to connect a thermistor cable. After the thermistor is connected, the system can monitor the temperature of a selected area, such as critical motherboard components or nearby connected devices.

<PIC>

As shown in the image, the T_SENSOR1 header is a 2-pin connector located on the lower-right area of the motherboard. Its pin definition is:

- Pin 1: SENSOR IN
- Pin 2: GND

Please note that the thermistor cable is purchased separately. Before connecting or removing the cable, power off the system and unplug the power cord to avoid electrical damage.

Once the thermistor cable is installed, you can check its reading in BIOS under the Monitor menu as T_Sensor Temperature. The same sensor can also be selected as a temperature source for fan control, including chassis fan or extension fan control, so the fan speed can respond to the temperature detected by the external thermal sensor.,['Manual25_43']

To use Quick Release (QR/QPR) on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, wait until the pressure cooking program has finished, then press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once the button is locked in Vent, steam will release forcefully from the top of the steam release valve. This is expected during Quick Release. Keep the cooker in a clear, open area and do not cover or block the steam release valve while it is venting.

<PIC>

When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. Only open the lid after the float valve is fully down. Do not try to force the lid open while the float valve is raised, as the cooker is still pressurized.

Important safety notes:
- Steam is very hot. Keep hands, face, arms, and any exposed skin away from the steam release valve during release.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, foamy, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chili, pasta, oatmeal, congee, or thick sauces.
- Do not use Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains. For these foods, use Natural Release instead to reduce splatter and help the pressure come down more gradually.,['multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, make sure you are using the pressure cooking lid, not the air fryer lid. The pressure cooking lid automatically sets to “Seal” when it is closed, and the cooker will play a jingle when the lid is opened or closed correctly. During non-pressure cooking, the lid can be removed; once the cooker becomes pressurized, the lid locks in place for safety.

Closing the pressure cooking lid:

1. Hold the lid by the handle and line up the open/unlocked position marker on the lid with the matching marker on the cooker base.
2. Lower the lid straight down onto the lid track so it sits evenly on the cooker base.
3. Turn the lid clockwise until the lid marker lines up with the closed/locked marker on the cooker base. Once aligned, the lid is in the correct closed position.

<PIC>

Removing the pressure cooking lid:

1. Hold the lid handle firmly and turn the lid counterclockwise until the lid marker lines up with the open/unlocked marker on the rim of the cooker base.
2. Lift the lid up and away from the cooker base, tilting it slightly toward you as you remove it.

<PIC>

Important safety note: never try to remove the lid while the float valve is raised. A raised float valve means the cooker is still pressurized. Wait until the float valve drops down completely before opening the lid, and never force the lid open.,['Manual30_13', 'Manual30_12']

To install or reset the float valve on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. From the top of the lid, insert the narrow end of the float valve down into the float valve hole. The flat top of the float valve should remain visible on the top surface of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep one finger on the flat top of the float valve to hold it in place, then carefully turn the lid over.

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom end of the float valve until it is securely attached. The silicone cap helps the float valve seal properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Before using the pressure cooking function, check that both parts are fitted correctly: the float valve should move freely up and down, and the silicone cap should be attached securely underneath the lid.

For safety, do not operate the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing, loose, or incorrectly installed. Also, do not touch the float valve during cooking, and never try to open the lid while the float valve is raised, as this means the cooker is still pressurized.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The anti-block shield is a key safety part on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. It helps stop food particles from entering the steam release pipe, which supports proper steam flow and pressure regulation during pressure cooking. Because it is part of the pressure-control system, the cooker should not be used for pressure cooking unless the anti-block shield is correctly installed.

<PIC>

Before each use of the pressure cooking lid, please check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and not clogged. This is especially important after cooking foods that foam, expand, or contain starch, such as rice, beans, pasta, oatmeal, soups, stews, or congee.

To remove the anti-block shield for cleaning, hold the lid securely like a steering wheel. Using both thumbs, press firmly against the side of the anti-block shield, pushing toward the side of the lid and upward until it pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

After cleaning, reinstall it before pressure cooking. Place the anti-block shield back over the prongs, then press down firmly until it snaps into position. Once installed, it should sit securely in place.

<PIC>

Please do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed, as it is necessary for safe pressure cooking and proper pressure regulation.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_34', 'Manual30_35']

The steam release valve is controlled by the quick release button on the pressure cooking lid. This button determines whether the cooker is sealed for pressure cooking or venting to release pressure.

<PIC>

To set the steam release valve, use the quick release button as follows:

1. Seal position
Leave the quick release button popped up. When the pressure cooking lid is closed correctly, it is designed to automatically set to Seal.

If you need to return it to Seal, use a quick flicking motion. Although the lid may say “Turn to Seal,” you should not rotate the quick release button more than 45°. Let it spring back to its original raised position.

Use Seal when pressure cooking or when allowing pressure to release naturally after cooking.

2. Vent position
Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into place. This sets the steam release valve to Vent and allows pressure to release quickly.

<PIC>

When the button is locked down in the Vent position, steam will release in a strong upward jet from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during quick release.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders: do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve. The steam released is very hot, so keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the valve while pressure is being released. Also, do not try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped, which indicates the cooker is no longer pressurized.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set/install the anti-block shield on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, first make sure the pressure cooking lid is cool, clean, and removed from the cooker base.

Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid. Locate the steam release pipe area and the small metal prongs where the anti-block shield fits.

Place the anti-block shield directly over the prongs, then press it down firmly until it snaps securely into position. The shield should sit flat and should not feel loose.

<PIC>

The anti-block shield helps prevent food particles from entering the steam release pipe, so it is an important safety part for pressure cooking. Please do not operate the pressure cooker unless the anti-block shield is properly installed.,['Manual30_35']

To install the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. From the top of the pressure cooking lid, insert the narrow end of the float valve down into the float valve hole. The flat top of the float valve should remain visible on the top side of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep one finger on the flat top of the float valve to hold it in place, then carefully turn the lid over so you can access the underside of the lid.

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom end of the float valve. Make sure the cap is fully seated and secure, so the float valve can move correctly and seal properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

4. Before using the pressure cooking function, check that both parts are installed correctly: the float valve should sit in the lid opening, and the silicone cap should be attached underneath.

Important: Do not operate the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing, loose, or not properly installed. These parts work together to help seal in pressurized steam. Also, do not touch the float valve during cooking, and only open the lid after the float valve has dropped back down, indicating that pressure has been released.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The condensation collector is installed on the back of the cooker base. Its purpose is to catch any liquid that may overflow from the condensation rim during cooking, so it should be fitted before you start using the cooker.

To install it, locate the tabs on the back of the cooker base and the matching grooves on the condensation collector. Align the grooves over the tabs, then slide the condensation collector straight into place until it sits securely against the cooker base.

<PIC>

After each use, remove the condensation collector by pulling it straight away from the cooker base. Empty any collected liquid, rinse it out, and let it dry before reinstalling it for the next use.,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so the underside is facing up.
2. Position the silicone sealing ring around the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.
3. Starting at one section, press the sealing ring down and behind the rack. Continue working your way around the entire lid until the ring is fully seated.
4. Press firmly all the way around the ring to make sure it is smooth and evenly fitted. There should be no puckering, twisting, or loose sections.

<PIC>

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and should stay in place when the lid is turned over. If any part of the ring pops out, looks wrinkled, or hangs loose, remove it and reinstall it before cooking.

Before each use, check that the sealing ring is clean, properly installed, and free from cuts, stretching, deformation, or other damage. Only use an authorized sealing ring made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a stretched or damaged sealing ring, as this may prevent the lid from sealing properly and could cause steam or food to escape during pressure cooking.,['Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from the pressure cooking lid, first make sure the cooker is unplugged, fully depressurized, and cooled to room temperature.

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the silicone sealing ring seated behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.
3. Grip the edge of the silicone ring with your fingers.
4. Gently pull the sealing ring outward and away from behind the stainless-steel rack, working it out a little at a time around the circle if needed.

<PIC>

After the sealing ring is removed, check the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. It should be secure, centered, and the same height all the way around the lid. If the rack looks bent, loose, uneven, or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

Tip: The sealing ring is usually easiest to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy. While it is removed, also check the ring for cuts, stretching, deformation, or damage. Do not use a damaged or stretched sealing ring, as it may affect pressure sealing and cause steam or food to leak during cooking.,['Manual30_30']

### Sealing ring

The sealing ring is the silicone ring fitted on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. When the lid is closed, it forms an air-tight seal between the lid and the inner pot, allowing the cooker to build and maintain pressure properly.

Before pressure cooking, make sure the sealing ring is installed correctly. Use only one sealing ring in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

Because silicone is porous, it can absorb strong aromas and certain flavors. If you often cook both savory and sweet dishes, or foods with strong smells, it is a good idea to keep extra sealing rings and use them separately to reduce flavor transfer.

### Important safety checks

Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged.

Before each use, please check that:

- The sealing ring has no cuts, cracks, deformation, or visible damage.
- The sealing ring is seated evenly around the stainless-steel sealing ring rack.
- The sealing ring is not puckered, twisted, loose, or hanging out of place.
- Only one sealing ring is installed.

Sealing rings naturally stretch over time with regular use. We recommend replacing the sealing ring every 12-18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, damage, or if steam leaks from the side of the lid.

If the sealing ring is not installed correctly or is damaged, food or steam may discharge from the cooker, which can cause injury or property damage.

### Removing the silicone sealing ring

To remove it, grip the edge of the silicone ring and gently pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack on the underside of the pressure cooking lid.

After removing the ring, check that the stainless-steel rack is secure, centered, and even all the way around the lid. If the rack appears bent or deformed, do not attempt to repair it yourself.

Tip: The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

<PIC>

### Installing the silicone sealing ring

Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack, then press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure it sits snugly behind the rack and does not pucker or bunch up.

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should stay in place and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If steam escapes steadily from the sides of the lid during pressure cooking, press Cancel, allow the cooker to depressurize safely, and check whether the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and properly installed before using the cooker again.,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is a key safety part on the pressure cooking lid of the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It sits on the steam release pipe and is required for pressure cooking. When pressure is released, hot steam comes out from the top of this valve.

<PIC>

Please note that the steam release valve is designed to sit loosely on the steam release pipe. This is normal. It should not be tightened or fixed in place, but it must be installed before using any pressure cooking function.

For safety, do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve. Do not lean over it, and keep your hands, face, and exposed skin away from the valve while the appliance is operating or while there may still be pressure inside.

The quick release button controls the steam release valve. In simple terms, it determines whether the cooker is sealed to hold pressure or venting to release pressure.

<PIC>

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once it is in the Vent position, a strong jet of hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is expected during Quick Release. Keep clear of the steam until pressure has fully released and the float valve has dropped.

<PIC>

For cleaning or checking the part, you can remove the steam release valve by pulling it straight up and off the steam release pipe.

<PIC>

To reinstall it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When installed correctly, it will still feel loose, but it should stay in place when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

Before each use, check that the steam release valve and steam release pipe are clean and not blocked. If steam escapes continuously from the steam release valve for longer than expected, or if the valve does not appear to be seated properly, turn the appliance off, allow it to depressurize safely, and check that all pressure control parts are clean and correctly installed.,['Manual30_15', 'Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is the control that operates the steam release valve on the pressure cooking lid. In simple terms, it determines whether the cooker stays sealed to build/hold pressure, or vents steam to release pressure.

<PIC>

There are two key positions:

1. Seal position: the button is popped up. Use this when pressure cooking so the cooker can build and maintain pressure.
2. Vent position: the button is pressed down and locked. Use this when you need a Quick Release after pressure cooking.

What makes it convenient is that you can release pressure without placing your hand directly over the steam outlet, helping keep your hands farther away from hot steam.

One important note: although the lid marking may say “Turn to Seal,” the action is more like a small flick. Do not rotate the quick release button more than about 1/4 inch or 45 degrees. After flicking it back to Seal, let it spring back into its normal raised position.

For Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once it is in Vent, steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. The steam may come out forcefully at first, and the cooker is depressurizing during this process.

<PIC>

When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. Only open the lid after the float valve has dropped. Never force the lid open while the float valve is still raised.

Safety reminder: the steam released from the steam release valve is very hot. Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve while releasing pressure, and do not cover or block the valve during use.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, is a pressure-release method where the cooker is left to cool down on its own after pressure cooking finishes. As the temperature inside the cooker gradually drops, the internal pressure decreases naturally over time.

<PIC>

To use Natural Release:
1. When cooking is complete, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position.
2. Turn Keep Warm off, unless your recipe specifically says otherwise.
3. Wait until the cooker has fully depressurized.
4. Open the lid only after the float valve has dropped down into the lid.

What you can expect:
- The food will continue to cook while pressure is being released. This is often called carryover cooking or resting.
- The release time can vary depending on the amount and type of food inside, and it may take up to about 40 minutes.
- Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop, indicating that the lid can be opened safely.

Natural Release is especially recommended after cooking high-starch or thick foods, such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, and congee. It is also the preferred method for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains, because it helps reduce splattering and foaming through the steam release valve.

For safety, do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still up. Never force the lid open, as the cooker may still be under pressure.,['Manual30_9']

The vacuum’s robot anatomy can be understood in three main areas: the top-side components, the control buttons and status indicators, and the underside cleaning/charging components. Top view From the top, the main visible parts are: - iAdapt® Localization Camera: Helps the robot recognize and navigate its surroundings. - Handle: Used for lifting or carrying the robot when needed. - Bin Release Button: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying or maintenance. Buttons and indicators The control panel includes three main buttons: - DOCK Button: Sends the robot back to its charging dock. - CLEAN Button: Wakes the robot, starts, pauses, or resumes a cleaning cycle. - SPOT Button: Starts spot cleaning for a concentrated area of debris. The status indicators show important operating information: - Wi-Fi® Indicator: Shows Wi-Fi connection status. - Troubleshooting Indicator: Lights when the robot needs attention or has an error. - Battery Indicator: Shows battery or charging status. - Full Bin Indicator: Lets you know when the dust bin needs to be emptied. - Dirt Detect™ Indicator: Lights when the robot detects a dirtier area and cleans it more thoroughly. Bottom view On the underside, you’ll find the parts that handle cleaning, movement, sensing, and charging: - Debris Extractors: The dual rollers that lift dirt, hair, and debris from the floor. - Extractor Caps: End caps on the extractors that may need cleaning if hair or debris collects around them. - Extractor Frame: Holds the extractors in place. - Extractor Frame Release Tabs: Used to open the extractor frame for cleaning or maintenance. - Dust Bin: Collects the debris picked up during cleaning. - Side Brush: Sweeps dirt from edges and corners into the robot’s cleaning path. - Charging Contacts: Connect with the charging dock so the robot can recharge. - Floor Tracking Sensor: Helps the robot track movement across the floor and navigate accurately.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The two primary modes available are Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode. Both are set using the switch on the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier, allowing you to guide where the vacuum should clean and which areas it should avoid. 1. Halo Mode
When the switch is moved to the down position, the device enters Halo Mode. In this mode, it creates an invisible protective zone around the device that the vacuum will not enter. This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, or delicate objects, and it can also help keep the vacuum out of small areas such as corners or spaces under a desk. The protective halo reaches approximately 24 inches from the center of the device. 2. Virtual Wall Mode
When the switch is moved to the up position, the device works as a Virtual Wall. It creates an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that the vacuum recognizes and will not cross. This mode is ideal for blocking doorways, room entrances, or other openings, and it can block spaces up to approximately 10 feet wide. The barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device. In short, use Halo Mode to protect a specific object or small area, and use Virtual Wall Mode to block the vacuum from entering a larger space or crossing an opening.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

To empty the vacuum cleaner's bin, please follow these steps:

1. Press the bin release button and pull the dust bin out of the vacuum. The release button is located on the bin area, as shown below.

<PIC>

2. Hold the bin over a trash container, then open the bin door to let the debris fall out. Tap the bin gently if needed to remove remaining dust or dirt.

<PIC>

After emptying, close the bin door securely and reinstall the bin into the vacuum until it clicks into place.

For best performance, empty the bin after each use and clean the filter regularly. If the full bin indicator turns on during a cleaning job, pause the vacuum, empty the bin, reinstall it, and then resume cleaning. If the indicator remains on even when the bin does not look full, the full bin sensors may need to be cleaned.,['Manual32_8', 'Manual32_9']

To clean the vacuum cleaner filter, please follow these steps:

1. Remove and open the dust bin, then take out the filter by holding the yellow tab. The yellow tab is the pull point for the filter.

<PIC>

2. Hold the filter over a trash container and gently tap it against the container to shake off dust and debris. Do not wash the filter with water; just tap out the loose debris.

<PIC>

3. Reinsert the filter after cleaning. Make sure the yellow tab is facing up when you put it back in place.

Important: The filter door will not close unless the filter is correctly reinstalled.

For best performance, clean the filter once per week. If you have pets at home, clean it twice per week. The filter should be replaced every 2 months.,['Manual32_10', 'Manual32_11']

To clean the full bin sensors, use a clean, dry cloth only. Do not spray liquid or cleaning solution onto the sensors or into the sensor openings.

1. Remove the dust bin from the vacuum and empty it completely. Press the bin release button, pull out the bin, then open the bin door and dispose of the debris.

<PIC>

2. With the bin removed, locate the full bin sensors inside the bin compartment of the vacuum. Gently wipe both sensors with a clean, dry cloth to remove dust or buildup that may affect detection.

<PIC>

3. Clean the sensor ports on the dust bin itself. Wipe both the inner sensor ports and the outer sensor ports with a clean, dry cloth, making sure the openings are clear and free of dust.

<PIC>

After cleaning, reinstall the bin securely before starting or resuming a cleaning cycle. If the full bin indicator was on even though the bin was not full, cleaning these sensors and ports should help restore proper detection.,['Manual32_12', 'Manual32_13', 'Manual32_14']

To clean the extractors, first turn the vacuum over and make sure it is not running. Then follow these steps:

1. Pinch the yellow extractor frame release tabs, then lift the extractor frame open. Check the exposed area and remove any visible obstructions, such as tangled hair, string, or larger debris.

<PIC>

2. Lift out both extractors. Remove the yellow extractor caps from the ends, then clear away any hair or debris wrapped under the caps or around the metal axles. Once clean, reinstall the extractor caps securely.

<PIC>

3. Check the opposite ends of the extractors. Remove hair, lint, or debris from the square and hexagonal plastic pegs so the rollers can rotate properly.

<PIC>

4. Before putting the extractors back, clear the vacuum path inside the extractor compartment. Make sure nothing is blocking the airflow path or lodged inside the cleaning head area.

<PIC>

5. Reinstall the extractors. Match each extractor peg by both color and shape with the corresponding icons on the cleaning head module, then close the extractor frame until it is seated properly.

For best performance, clean the extractors about once every 4 months, or about once every 3 months if you have pets. Replace them when they become worn, typically every 6-12 months.,['Manual32_19', 'Manual32_20', 'Manual32_21', 'Manual32_22']

To clean the sensors and charging contacts, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over and locate the sensors on the underside of the unit. Gently wipe each sensor area with a clean, dry cloth to remove dust or buildup. Do not spray cleaning solution, water, or any liquid directly onto the sensors or into the sensor openings, as this may damage the electronics.

<PIC>

2. Next, clean the charging contacts. Wipe the charging contacts on the vacuum and the charging contacts on the Home Base with a clean, dry cloth. Make sure both sets of contacts are free of dust, debris, or residue so the vacuum can dock and charge properly.

<PIC>

For best performance, clean the sensors and charging contacts about once per month, or more often if the vacuum is used in a dusty environment.,['Manual32_17', 'Manual32_18']

To position the vacuum cleaner’s Home Base correctly, place it in an open, uncluttered area where the vacuum can easily leave, return, and dock without obstruction.

Please follow these spacing requirements:

1. Leave at least 1.5 ft / 0.5 m of clear space on both the left and right sides of the Home Base.
2. Leave at least 4 ft / 1.2 m of clear space in front of the Home Base.
3. Keep the Home Base at least 4 ft / 1.2 m away from stairs or drop-offs.
4. Keep it at least 8 ft / 2.4 m away from virtual wall barriers, as these may interfere with docking or navigation.

<PIC>

Also, keep the Home Base plugged in at all times. For best performance, choose a location with consistent Wi-Fi coverage so the vacuum can stay connected to the app, receive updates, and return to its base reliably after cleaning.,['Manual32_0']

To clean the front caster wheel, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over and locate the front caster wheel. Pull the front wheel straight out firmly to remove the wheel assembly from the housing.

<PIC>

2. Check the wheel cavity and remove any dust, hair, string, or other debris from inside the opening.

3. Spin the wheel by hand to make sure it rotates freely. If the wheel feels stuck, tight, or does not spin smoothly, remove the wheel from its housing, then push firmly to remove the axle.

4. Clear away any hair or debris wrapped around the wheel or axle. Make sure the axle area is clean before reassembling.

5. Reinstall the axle, wheel, and housing parts. Push the front caster wheel assembly back into the vacuum until it clicks securely into place.

After reinstalling it, spin the wheel again briefly to confirm it moves freely and is seated properly.,['Manual32_15']

To clean the side brush, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over so you can access the side brush on the bottom of the unit. Using a coin or a small screwdriver, loosen and remove the screw located in the center of the side brush.

<PIC>

2. Lift the side brush off the brush post. Remove any hair, dust, or debris from both the brush and the brush post area.

3. Once everything is clean, place the side brush back onto the post and tighten the screw securely. Make sure the brush is properly seated and can spin freely before using the vacuum again.,['Manual32_16']

The dual-mode virtual wall barrier is used to control where the vacuum can and cannot clean. It creates an invisible boundary that the vacuum recognizes, helping keep it inside the areas you want cleaned and away from areas or objects you want to protect.

You can choose one of two modes depending on your cleaning needs. The switch on the device controls the mode: up for Virtual Wall Mode, down for Halo Mode, and the middle position turns the device off.

<PIC>

Halo Mode is best for protecting specific objects or small areas. When the switch is in the down position, the device creates an invisible circular protective zone around itself. The vacuum will turn away when it reaches this zone, helping prevent it from bumping into items such as pet bowls, vases, or other objects you do not want disturbed. The protected area reaches approximately 24 inches from the center of the device, creating about a 4-foot diameter zone.

<PIC>

Virtual Wall Mode is best for blocking doorways, room entrances, or open spaces. When the switch is in the up position, the device projects an invisible cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect. This barrier can block openings up to approximately 10 feet, so the vacuum stays on one side and does not cross into the restricted area. The barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device.

<PIC>

In short, use Halo Mode to protect items in a small surrounding area, and use Virtual Wall Mode to block the vacuum from entering a room or crossing an opening. If you will not use the barrier for an extended period, switch it to the off/middle position to help preserve battery life.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum detects a problem, it will alert you in three ways: it will play a two-tone distress sound, follow it with a spoken message explaining the issue, and the troubleshooting indicator will blink. You can locate the troubleshooting indicator on the control panel, shown with the warning triangle icon above the buttons.

<PIC>

Please listen carefully to the spoken message first, as it usually points to the area that needs attention. After checking and clearing the issue, try starting the vacuum again.

For some errors, rebooting the vacuum may resolve the problem. To reboot it:

1. Press and hold the CLEAN button for about 10 seconds.
2. Keep holding until all indicators illuminate.
3. Release the CLEAN button.
4. Wait for the audible tone, which confirms the reboot was successful.

If you use the scheduling feature, open the app after rebooting and confirm that your cleaning schedule is still set correctly. You can also use the app for more detailed support, current vacuum status, and troubleshooting videos.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera or equipment on a drop ceiling T-rail, use the included T-rail mounting hardware. The hardware is designed to fit most 9/16", 15/16", or 1 1/2" T-rails.

1. Set the T-rail clip spacing.
Use the dashed lines on the mount plate template to determine the correct spacing for the T-rail clips. Align the clips with the spacing guide before securing them.

<PIC>

2. Secure the T-rail clip set screws.
Once the clips are positioned, tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips. Use the included 5/64" (2 mm) hex key to secure them firmly.

<PIC>

3. Confirm the set screw locations.
The set screws are located on the T-rail clips as shown. Make sure they are tightened enough to keep the clips stable before attaching the mount plate.

<PIC>

4. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips.
Position the mount plate beneath the T-rail clips and fasten it using the mount plate holes marked with “G”. For standard T-rail mounting, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.

<PIC>

5. Snap the T-rail clips onto the T-rail.
Rotate the T-rail clips onto the T-rail and snap them into place. After installation, the black foam pads should be slightly compressed, which helps ensure a snug fit against the rail.

For recessed T-rail installations, use the modified hardware arrangement shown below: install the rubber spacers between the mount plate and the T-rail clips, and use the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws instead of the standard 4 mm screws.

<PIC>

After the mount plate is secured to the T-rail, you can continue with the camera installation by routing the Ethernet/PoE cable as needed and attaching the camera body to the mount plate.,['Manual33_10', 'Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_14']

To power the camera, turn the power switch from <OFF> to either <ON> or the < /> position.

<PIC>

Power switch positions:

<OFF>: The camera is turned off and will not operate.

<ON>: The camera is powered on and ready to use.

< />: The camera is powered on, and the camera’s dial operation is also enabled.

After switching the camera on, you can begin using it normally. If the camera is left unused for about 1 minute, it will automatically turn off to save battery power. To wake it again, press the shutter button halfway.

<PIC>

You can also change the auto power-off time in the camera menu under the Auto power off setting.,['Manual10_29', 'Manual10_30']

The brake lever is used to slow or stop the machine by braking the entire drive system. To stop, first release the throttle, then squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar. When the brake is applied, the brake light will illuminate.

<PIC>

When slowing down or stopping, apply the brake gently and progressively, not suddenly. Sudden or excessive braking can cause the drive track to lose traction, which may reduce control and increase the risk of an accident. On ice, hard-packed snow, or downhill sections, allow extra stopping distance and use light brake pressure as needed.

For the parking brake button, use it whenever the machine is parked or before starting the engine. To set the parking brake: squeeze the brake lever, push down the parking brake button, then release the brake lever while keeping the button engaged. This locks the brake in place.

<PIC>

To release the parking brake, simply squeeze the brake lever again; the parking brake button will disengage. Always confirm the parking brake is released before riding. Do not operate the machine with the parking brake applied, because this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40']

Before using a snowmobile, complete the following preparation checks to help ensure the machine is safe, ready, and suitable for operation:

1. Review the safety labels and become familiar with the controls.
Make sure you understand the location and function of the throttle, brake, steering, engine stop switch, parking brake, and other operating controls before starting the engine. If any control is unclear or does not operate as expected, do not ride until it has been checked.
<PIC>

2. Check throttle, brake, and steering operation before starting.
Confirm that the throttle lever moves smoothly and returns fully to the idle position when released. Also check that the brake responds properly and that the steering moves freely without excessive looseness, binding, or unusual resistance.
<PIC>
<PIC>

3. Apply the parking brake before starting the engine.
Set the parking brake before attempting to start the snowmobile. After the engine is running and you are ready to move, release it fully. Do not ride with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.

4. Check the fuel level and secure the fuel tank cap.
Make sure there is enough fuel for your ride. Refuel carefully, keep snow or ice out of the tank, and always close the fuel tank cap securely after refueling. Wipe up any spilled fuel immediately.
<PIC>

5. Check the engine oil level.
Verify that there is sufficient oil in the oil tank and that the level is within the acceptable range. Add the recommended oil if needed before operating the snowmobile.
<PIC>

6. Inspect the manual starter rope.
Check the starter rope for fraying, wear, cuts, or other damage. A damaged rope can fail during starting, so it should be repaired or replaced before use.
<PIC>

7. Inspect the drive and running components.
Do not operate the engine without the drive belt or drive belt guard installed. Before riding, check the V-belt, drive V-belt guard, drive track, slide runners, skis and ski runners, lights, battery, fittings, fasteners, and the availability of service tools and spare parts. For longer trips, it is also wise to carry extra fuel and oil.

8. Wear proper protective clothing.
Use an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, snowmobile suit, boots, and snowmobile gloves or mittens that still allow proper thumb and finger control.
<PIC>

9. Confirm safe operating conditions and rider readiness.
Do not operate the snowmobile after drinking alcohol or taking drugs. If the snowmobile is designed for the operator only, do not carry passengers, as doing so can affect balance and control.
<PIC>
<PIC>

10. Make a final safety check before moving off.
Keep bystanders away from the rear of the machine when starting, inspecting, or adjusting it. Confirm the area ahead is clear, the machine is on suitable snow or ice, and all controls respond normally before beginning your ride.,['Manual34_7', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_99', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_11', 'snowmobile_03', 'snowmobile_02']

Before starting the snowmobile, please complete these safety checks first:

- Make sure the parking brake is applied.
- Confirm the engine stop switch is in the “ON/RUN” position.
- Check that the throttle, brake, and steering operate normally.
- Start the engine only in a well-ventilated area. Do not run the engine in an enclosed space.

<PIC>

1. Set the starter lever, also called the choke, according to engine temperature:

- Fully open: use this for starting a cold engine.
- Half-open: use this while warming up the engine on CS340E, ET410TR, and VK540E models.
- Close: use this for starting a warm engine.

For a recoil-start model:

2. Turn the main switch to the “ON” position.

3. Pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage, then pull it briskly to start the engine.

<PIC>

4. After the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.

5. Let the engine warm up. When the engine runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall, return the starter lever/choke to the “Close” position.

For an electric-start model:

2. Turn the main switch to the “START” position.

<PIC>

3. As soon as the engine starts, release the switch immediately.

4. Move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.

5. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not begin to stall when the starter lever/choke is returned to the “Close” position.

Important: If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and try again. Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to help preserve the battery. Do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds in any single attempt.

Once the engine has warmed up properly, release the parking brake before riding. Never drive the snowmobile with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake and reduce braking performance.,['Manual34_111', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

A V-Belt Holder is the dedicated holder used to store a spare V-belt on the machine, so the spare belt is available for emergency replacement when needed.

To use it, place the spare V-belt into the holder provided. If you need to remove the spare V-belt, loosen bolt ① first, then take the belt out of the holder.

<PIC>

When reinstalling the spare V-belt, make sure bolt ① is tightened securely after the belt is placed back into the holder. Also check that the belt sits correctly in the holder and that nearby hoses are not pinched or displaced during installation.

<PIC>

CAUTION: The V-belt must be secured firmly in the holder. If it comes loose, it may contact the hot muffler and become damaged.

<PIC>,['Manual34_51', 'Manual34_52', 'Manual34_55']

Before adjusting the throttle cable, make sure the engine idle speed has already been correctly adjusted. Also confirm that the throttle lever moves smoothly before starting the adjustment.

1. Loosen the throttle cable adjuster locknut.

2. Turn the adjuster in or out until the throttle lever free play is within the proper specification:

<PIC>

For the adjustment area, use the following reference points:
- ① Throttle lever free play
- ② Locknut
- ③ Adjuster

<PIC>

When checking the free play, lightly move or pull the throttle cable/throttle lever as shown, then adjust until the specified free play is reached.

<PIC>

3. After the free play is correct, tighten the locknut securely so the adjustment does not change.

After tightening, recheck the throttle lever free play and make sure the throttle operates smoothly and returns properly.,['Manual34_148', 'Manual34_149', 'Manual34_150']

Before riding, always confirm that the steering system is working properly. Before starting the engine, check the throttle, brake, and steering for normal operation. For the steering system specifically, make sure there is no excessive play or looseness at the handlebars, skis, or steering column. If the handlebars or skis move abnormally, or the steering feels loose, do not operate the snowmobile until it has been inspected and corrected.

<PIC>

When riding, always keep both hands on the handlebars and keep your feet on the running boards. To steer, turn the handlebar in the direction you want to go. Use smooth, controlled handlebar input rather than sudden movements.

For most snow surfaces, proper body positioning is essential. As you approach a turn, slow down first, then begin turning the handlebars in the desired direction. At the same time, shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn. This body movement helps the snowmobile stay balanced and improves steering control.

<PIC>

Practice this turning technique at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. As your skill improves, you can apply the same technique at slightly higher speeds or in tighter turns. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you will need to lean into the turn.

Avoid abrupt throttle changes, hard braking, incorrect body movement, or entering a turn too fast. These actions can reduce control and may cause the snowmobile to tip. If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer slightly toward the outside of the turn.

On ice or icy surfaces, drive slowly and cautiously. Steering response is greatly reduced on ice, and rapid acceleration, sudden turning, or hard braking can easily cause an uncontrolled spin.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

For most snow surfaces, the key to turning a snowmobile is proper body positioning, often called “body English.” Your body weight and balance help the skis and track stay controlled through the turn.

Follow these steps when approaching and making a turn:

1. Slow down before the curve. Reduce your speed early so you enter the turn under control.

2. Begin turning the handlebars in the direction you want to go.

3. Shift your weight to the inside running board of the turn. For example, when turning left, put more weight on the left running board.

4. Lean your upper body into the turn. This helps balance the snowmobile and reduces the chance of tipping.

<PIC>

Practice this technique many times at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. Once you are comfortable, you can gradually use the same technique at higher speeds or in tighter turns. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you will need to lean into the turn.

Avoid actions that can make the snowmobile unstable, such as sudden throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movement, or entering a turn too fast for how sharp it is. These can cause the snowmobile to tip.

If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to help regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn.

Important: Do not ride at higher speeds until you are fully familiar with your snowmobile’s handling and controls.,['Manual34_127']

When riding uphill on a snowmobile, build your skill gradually and use smooth, controlled movements. Start by practicing on gentle slopes, and only attempt steeper or more difficult climbs after you are comfortable with throttle control, braking, balance, and body positioning.

Before starting the climb, approach the hill with enough momentum by accelerating before you reach the slope. Once the snowmobile begins climbing, reduce the throttle opening slightly to help prevent the track from spinning or slipping. Avoid sudden throttle changes, because they can reduce traction and make the snowmobile harder to control.

Keep your body weight on the uphill side at all times. When climbing straight up a hill, lean forward to help keep the skis and track properly loaded. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars to maintain balance and improve control.

<PIC>

As you approach the top of the hill, slow down and stay alert. Be prepared for obstacles, sharp drop-offs, other riders, vehicles, or changing terrain on the other side of the crest.

If the snowmobile cannot continue climbing, do not spin the track. Stop the engine and set the parking brake. Then, from the uphill side, pull the rear of the snowmobile around until the machine points back down the hill. Do not stand or move on the downhill side of the snowmobile. Once it is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

Warning: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginners or novice snowmobile riders.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, the key is to stay slow, controlled, and balanced throughout the descent.

1. Keep your speed to a minimum before and during the downhill run. Do not let the snowmobile build speed freely as you descend.

2. Apply a small amount of throttle, just enough to keep the clutch engaged. This allows engine compression to help slow the machine and prevents the snowmobile from rolling freely down the hill.

<PIC>

3. Use the brake lightly and frequently. Apply gentle pressure in short, controlled applications rather than braking hard all at once.

4. Maintain a stable riding position and stay prepared to adjust your balance as the slope or snow conditions change.

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking during a descent. Excessive braking can lock the track, which may cause loss of control. Always brake gently and plan your speed early, especially on ice or hard-packed snow where stopping distances are much longer.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing the face of a slope, also called side hilling, requires careful body positioning and should not be attempted by beginner or novice snowmobile riders. Practice on easier terrain first, and avoid steep or icy slopes until you have the skill to control balance and traction confidently.

When crossing a slope, keep your body weight shifted toward the uphill side of the snowmobile. This helps counter the tendency of the machine to lean or slide downhill. A recommended position is to kneel with the knee of your downhill leg on the seat while keeping the foot of your uphill leg on the running board. This stance gives you a stronger, more flexible position so you can quickly shift your weight as the terrain changes.

<PIC>

The image shows the correct uphill-weighted riding posture: the rider leans toward the higher side of the slope, with one knee on the seat and the uphill foot planted on the running board. This position helps maintain balance while crossing the slope and makes it easier to react if the snowmobile begins to slide or tip.

Because snow and ice can be slippery, be prepared for the snowmobile to slide sideways. If it begins to slide and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. Once the machine regains balance and traction, gradually steer back toward your intended direction of travel.

If the snowmobile starts to tip, steer down the hill to help regain balance. If you cannot maintain control and the snowmobile is going to tip over, dismount immediately on the uphill side to avoid being caught on the downhill side of the machine.,['Manual34_129']

To inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile, follow these steps:

1. Make sure the engine is off and has cooled down before touching any engine components. The engine and muffler can become very hot after operation.

2. Remove the spark plug periodically and inspect its condition. Heat and deposits gradually wear down the spark plug, so regular inspection helps prevent poor starting, misfiring, plug fouling, or low engine power.

3. Check the color of the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. For a snowmobile being ridden under normal conditions, the ideal color is medium to light tan.

4. If the spark plug color is clearly different from normal, it may indicate an engine or fuel/air mixture issue. For example, a very white center electrode porcelain area may point to an intake air leak or carburetion problem for that cylinder. In this case, do not try to diagnose the issue yourself; have the snowmobile checked by a dealer.

5. Confirm that the spark plug has the correct thread length, also called reach. Using the wrong reach can cause engine problems:
- If the reach is too long, overheating and engine damage may occur.
- If the reach is too short, spark plug fouling and poor performance may occur. Carbon may also build up on exposed threads, causing hot spots and possible thread damage.

Use the correct spark plug reach for your model as shown below:
<PIC>

6. Before installing the spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust it to the specified value. In the illustration below, item 1 shows the spark plug reach, and item 2 shows the electrode gap:
<PIC>

7. Before reinstalling the spark plug, clean the gasket surface and wipe any grime from the threads.

8. Install the spark plug and tighten it to the proper torque.

Spark plug tightening torque: 28 Nm (2.8 m-kg, 20 ft-lb)

If the spark plug is dirty with carbon, wet, badly worn, or damaged, clean it if appropriate or replace it with the correct type. Consult a dealer before changing to a different spark plug type.,['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

To clean a snowmobile properly, follow these steps:

1. Thoroughly clean the entire machine, both inside and outside. This helps remove corrosive salts, acids, road residue, and other buildup that can damage metal parts and finishes over time.

2. Apply Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaning product, to areas with mud, grease, oil, or heavy grime. Allow it to loosen the dirt before washing, especially around the suspension, underbody, track area, and other hard-to-reach spots.

3. Wash the snowmobile with mild soap and water. Avoid using harsh chemicals, as they may damage painted surfaces, decals, rubber parts, or protective coatings.

4. Rinse the machine thoroughly with clean water to remove all soap and loosened residue. Make sure no cleaner remains in crevices, vents, or around moving parts.

5. Dry the snowmobile completely after rinsing. Pay special attention to metal components and recessed areas where moisture can remain, as trapped water may lead to corrosion.

Cleaning is especially important after riding or transporting the snowmobile in areas where road salt is used, since salt residue can quickly cause corrosion if left on the machine.

You can memorize or remove channels manually in two ways. The fastest method is to use the MEMORY/ERASE button, while the MENU method gives you more control through the on-screen channel settings.

Method 1: Using the MEMORY/ERASE button

1. Use the ▲/▼ channel buttons or the NUMBER buttons to select the channel you want to add or remove.
2. Press the MEMORY/ERASE button on the remote control.
3. Select Memory to save the channel, or Erase to remove it from the channel list.

<PIC>

After a channel is memorized, you can select it normally with the channel ▲/▼ buttons. If it is erased, it will be skipped when you browse channels with ▲/▼, though you may still be able to enter it directly with the NUMBER buttons.

Method 2: Using the MENU

1. Press MENU.
2. Use ▲/▼ until the CH. menu is displayed.
3. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to select Manual.
4. Press ▶ again, then use ▲/▼ to select Channel.
5. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to choose the channel number you want to memorize or erase.

<PIC>

6. Select Memory.
7. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to choose On or Off:
   - On: memorizes the selected channel.
   - Off: erases the selected channel from the memorized channel list.
8. Press MENU repeatedly to exit.

<PIC>

For everyday use, the MEMORY/ERASE button is the quickest option. Use the MENU method when you want to confirm the exact channel and memory setting on screen before saving the change.,['Manual35_9', 'Manual35_10', 'Manual35_11']

Common causes and conditions that can lead to poor television or radio reception include signal interference, weak antenna input, reflected signals, weathered antenna equipment, or issues with the broadcast/source itself. The most common symptoms and causes are as follows:

1. Ignition or electrical interference

When reception is affected by ignition-type interference, the picture may flutter, drift, show black spots, or display horizontal streaks. This is usually caused by nearby electrical sources such as automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, or other electrical appliances.

<PIC>

2. Ghosting or multiple images

Ghosting happens when the TV signal travels along two paths: one direct path to the antenna and another path reflected from tall buildings, hills, or other large objects. This can create a duplicated or shadow-like image on the screen. Adjusting the direction or position of the antenna may help improve reception.

<PIC>

3. Snow or speckled picture

A snowy picture usually appears when the receiver is in a weak or fringe signal area. The image may be covered with many small dots because the antenna signal is not strong enough. In this case, using a better-positioned antenna or installing a special outdoor antenna may improve picture quality.

<PIC>

Other possible causes include:

- The antenna signal is weak or unstable.
- Strong random signals from a nearby car, airplane, or other source interfere with the TV signal.
- The antenna or antenna cable is severely weathered, damaged, loose, or poorly connected.
- An old, damaged, or poor-quality recorded tape is being played through connected equipment.
- The broadcast station or selected channel has a temporary signal problem.

For poor reception, you can try the following checks:

- Try another channel to confirm whether the issue is limited to one station.
- Check that the antenna or cable connection is secure.
- Check for nearby sources of local interference, such as electrical tools or appliances.
- Adjust the antenna direction or position.
- Run Auto Program again if channels were stored while the signal quality was poor.
- If the picture shows horizontal stripes, twisting, or unstable color, use the Fine adjustment function for that channel.

<PIC>,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_45']

Closed captioning displays the audio portion of a TV program as written words on the screen, similar to subtitles. This can help you follow dialogue, narration, or other spoken content when captions are available from the broadcast.

<PIC>

You can turn captions or on-screen text on in two ways: by using the CAPTION button for quick selection, or by using the MENU button if you prefer to adjust the setting through the TV menu.

Quick method: using the CAPTION button

1. Press the CAPTION button on the remote control.
2. Press the CAPTION button repeatedly to cycle through the available options: Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
3. When the option you want is displayed, press the ENTER(O) button to exit.

<PIC>

What the options mean:

- Off: Turns captions or text display off.
- Mode1: Usually displays the main closed caption signal for a program.
- Mode2: May display a secondary caption signal, such as alternate captioning or program information, if provided by the broadcaster.
- Text1 / Text2: Displays text services when available, such as program lists, weather, news, or other full-screen text information.

Menu method: using the MENU button

1. Press the MENU button.
2. Use the up/down buttons to select the Special menu.
3. Press the right button to enter the Special menu.
4. Use the up/down buttons to select Caption.
5. Press the right button, then use the up/down buttons to choose Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
6. Press the MENU button repeatedly to exit the menu.

<PIC>

Please note: Not every broadcast includes caption or text signals. If captions do not appear after you select a caption mode, the program may not provide captions, or the TV signal may be too weak or affected by interference. The TV will remember the last caption mode you selected, even after the TV is turned off.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For the best signal reception, we recommend using an outdoor antenna. Before connecting it, inspect the antenna and cable carefully, since severe weathering, corrosion, loose connectors, or damaged cable can reduce signal quality.

<PIC>

You can connect the outdoor antenna in one of the following ways, depending on the cable type:

**If your antenna uses 300 ohm flat wire:**
1. Attach the two ends of the 300 ohm flat wire to the screw terminals on the 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter.
2. Tighten the screws so the wire is held securely.
3. Insert the end of the 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

**If your antenna uses a 75 ohm coaxial cable:**
1. Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.
2. Make sure the connector is firmly seated so the signal is stable.

After the antenna is connected, run **AUTO PROGRAM** so the TV can search for and store all active channels available in your area. You can do this by pressing the **AUTO PRG.** button on the remote control, or by opening the channel menu and selecting **Auto prog.**

If the picture is still unclear after setup, please check the following:
- Confirm the antenna cable is connected securely.
- Check whether there is local electrical interference nearby.
- Adjust the antenna’s direction or position.
- Run AUTO PROGRAM again if the signal quality changes after repositioning the antenna.

Changing the antenna direction or location can often improve reception, especially when you see ghosting, multiple images, snowy picture, or unstable reception.,['Manual35_39']

To connect a DVD player to the TV for the best picture and sound quality, use the COMPONENT DVD INPUT connection and match each cable by its label and color.

1. Connect the component video cables from the DVD player to the TV.

On the DVD player, connect the video output jacks to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i) jacks as follows:

- Y output on the DVD player to Y input on the TV
- PB output on the DVD player to PB input on the TV
- PR output on the DVD player to PR input on the TV

These three cables carry the video signal, so each one must be connected to the matching jack. If any of them are swapped, the picture may appear distorted, discolored, or may not display correctly.

2. Connect the audio cables from the DVD player to the TV.

Connect the DVD player’s audio output jacks to the TV’s audio input jacks:

- L audio output to Audio L input
- R audio output to Audio R input

This provides left and right stereo sound through the TV speakers.

<PIC>

3. Check the label names on your DVD player if they are different.

Some DVD players use different names for the same component video outputs. If your DVD player does not show PB or PR exactly, match the terminals this way:

- Y connects to Y
- Cb, B-Y, Pb, or PB connects to PB
- Cr, R-Y, Pr, or PR connects to PR

<PIC>

4. Select the correct input on the TV.

After all cables are connected, turn on the DVD player and the TV. Then press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control or on the TV front panel until COMPONENT is selected.

Once COMPONENT is selected, start playback on the DVD player. The DVD picture should appear on the TV, and the sound should play through the connected audio input.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

It is also helpful to perform a hard reset or restart the device to clear any temporary system glitches.
